title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of the two numbers that add up to target.
dry_run: nums=[2,7,11,15],target=9. HashMap: store 2→0. Check 9-7=2 found at 0. Return [0,1].
approach: Single pass hash map. For each num store complement→index. O(n) time O(n) space.
what_interviewers_want: Why O(n) over O(n²). Edge cases: duplicates, negative numbers, no solution.

title: 3Sum
description: Find all unique triplets in array that sum to zero.
dry_run: Sort [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]. Fix i=-4: l=-1,r=2 sum=-3<0 l++. … Fix i=-1: l=0,r=2 sum=1>0 r--. l=0,r=1 sum=0 ✓. Skip duplicates.
approach: Sort + fix one + two-pointer inner. Skip duplicates explicitly. O(n²) time.
what_interviewers_want: Deduplication of both outer and inner pointer. Why sorting helps. Can you do O(n²) without extra space?
python_code:
def threeSum(nums):
    nums.sort()
    res = []
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue
        l, r = i+1, len(nums)-1
        while l < r:
            s = n + nums[l] + nums[r]
            if s == 0:
                res.append([n, nums[l], nums[r]])
                while l < r and nums[l] == nums[l+1]: l += 1
                while l < r and nums[r] == nums[r-1]: r -= 1
                l += 1; r -= 1
            elif s < 0: l += 1
            else: r -= 1
    return res

title: Container With Most Water
description: Find two lines forming container holding most water.
dry_run: height=[1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]. l=0,r=8. min(1,7)×8=8. Move shorter (l). l=1,r=8. min(8,7)×7=49. Move shorter (r). Continue…
approach: Two pointers from ends. Move the pointer with shorter height. Proof: moving taller can only decrease area.
what_interviewers_want: Prove why moving the taller pointer is suboptimal. What if all heights are equal?
python_code:
def maxArea(height):
    l, r = 0, len(height)-1
    res = 0
    while l < r:
        res = max(res, min(height[l], height[r]) * (r - l))
        if height[l] < height[r]: l += 1
        else: r -= 1
    return res

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Find length of longest substring without repeating characters.
dry_run: s='abcabcbb'. Window [a], [ab], [abc], hit 'a'→shrink to [bca], [bcab]→shrink [cab]… max=3.
approach: Sliding window with char→last_index map. Jump left pointer directly on collision. O(n).
what_interviewers_want: Jump vs increment left pointer — the map optimization. Unicode vs ASCII chars.

title: Minimum Window Substring
description: Find minimum window in s containing all characters of t.
dry_run: s='ADOBECODEBANC',t='ABC'. have=0,need=3. Expand until have==need, shrink left to minimize.
approach: Two freq maps + have/need counters. Only update have when freq exactly matches needed.
what_interviewers_want: The have/need counter trick — avoids map equality check on every step. O(n+m).
python_code:
def minWindow(s, t):
    if not t: return ''
    need = Counter(t)
    have, required = 0, len(need)
    l, res, res_len = 0, [-1,-1], float('inf')
    window = {}
    for r, c in enumerate(s):
        window[c] = window.get(c,0)+1
        if c in need and window[c] == need[c]: have += 1
        while have == required:
            if (r-l+1) < res_len:
                res_len = r-l+1; res = [l,r]
            window[s[l]] -= 1
            if s[l] in need and window[s[l]] < need[s[l]]: have -= 1
            l += 1
    l,r = res
    return s[l:r+1] if res_len != float('inf') else ''

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Find maximum in every window of size k as it slides across array.
dry_run: nums=[1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7],k=3. Deque stores indices of useful elements. Window [1,3,-1]→3. [3,-1,-3]→3…
approach: Monotonic deque. Pop from back when smaller. Pop from front when out of window. O(n).
what_interviewers_want: Why deque over heap — O(n) vs O(n log k). The two-side pop logic. Real use: stream analytics.

title: Binary Search
description: Classic binary search on sorted array.
approach: lo+(hi-lo)//2 to avoid overflow. Loop while lo<=hi. O(log n).
what_interviewers_want: Overflow-safe midpoint. Loop vs recursive. When to use lo<hi vs lo<=hi.
python_code:
def search(nums, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = lo + (hi-lo)//2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: lo = mid+1
        else: hi = mid-1
    return -1

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Search target in rotated sorted array without knowing pivot.
dry_run: nums=[4,5,6,7,0,1,2],target=0. mid=7. Left half [4..7] sorted. 0 not in [4,7]→right. mid=1. 0 in [0,1]→left. Found.
approach: At each binary search step, determine which half is sorted. Check if target lies in sorted half.
what_interviewers_want: Identifying the sorted half is the key insight. Handle duplicates variant (LC 81).
python_code:
def search(nums, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = lo+(hi-lo)//2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        if nums[lo] <= nums[mid]:  # left sorted
            if nums[lo] <= target < nums[mid]: hi = mid-1
            else: lo = mid+1
        else:  # right sorted
            if nums[mid] < target <= nums[hi]: lo = mid+1
            else: hi = mid-1
    return -1

title: Koko Eating Bananas
description: Find minimum eating speed k to finish all bananas in h hours.
dry_run: piles=[3,6,7,11],h=8. Binary search k in [1,11]. k=6: ceil(3/6)+ceil(6/6)+ceil(7/6)+ceil(11/6)=1+1+2+2=6≤8✓. Try lower.
approach: Binary search on answer space [1, max(piles)]. canFinish(k) in O(n). Total O(n log m).
what_interviewers_want: Binary search on the answer, not the array. This pattern applies to many 'find minimum X' problems.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: Count distinct ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time.
dry_run: n=5. dp[1]=1,dp[2]=2,dp[3]=3,dp[4]=5,dp[5]=8. Fibonacci pattern.
approach: dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. Collapse to two vars for O(1) space.
what_interviewers_want: Recognize Fibonacci. Matrix exponentiation for O(log n). Generalize to k steps.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
description: Find length of LCS of two strings. Classic 2D DP problem.
dry_run: s1='abcde',s2='ace'. 2D table: when chars match, dp[i][j]=1+dp[i-1][j-1]. When not, max of left/top.
approach: dp[i][j] = LCS of s1[:i] and s2[:j]. O(mn) time. Space optimize to O(min(m,n)).
what_interviewers_want: Reconstruct actual LCS (backtrack through dp). Relate to Edit Distance and Diff algorithms.

title: Edit Distance
description: Find minimum operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert word1 to word2.
dry_run: word1='horse',word2='ros'. Build 2D dp. dp[i][j] = min ops for word1[:i] and word2[:j].
approach: If chars match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: 1+min(insert,delete,replace).
what_interviewers_want: All three operations in one formula. Connection to DNA sequence alignment. Space optimization.

title: Coin Change
description: Fewest coins to make amount. Unlimited coin supply.
approach: Bottom-up DP. dp[i]=min coins for amount i. O(amount×coins).
what_interviewers_want: Why greedy fails (coins=[1,3,4],amount=6→greedy=3,DP=2). Top-down vs bottom-up.

title: Word Break
description: Can string s be segmented into dictionary words?
dry_run: s='leetcode',dict=['leet','code']. dp[0..8]. dp[4]=True (leet). dp[8]=True (code).
approach: dp[i]=can we reach index i. For each i, try all j<i where dp[j] and s[j:i] in wordSet.
what_interviewers_want: Trie optimization for large dict. BFS approach. Print all possible segmentations (LC 140).

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Find length of longest strictly increasing subsequence.
dry_run: nums=[10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]. O(n²): dp=[1,1,1,2,2,3,4,4]. O(n log n): patience sorting with binary search.
approach: O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with binary search on tails array. Know both.
what_interviewers_want: O(n log n) with bisect — most candidates only know O(n²). Print actual subsequence.

title: 0/1 Knapsack
description: Maximize value fitting items in capacity W. Each item used at most once.
dry_run: weights=[1,3,4,5],values=[1,4,5,7],W=7. dp[i][w]=max(skip item i, take item i).
approach: dp[i][w]=max(dp[i-1][w], values[i]+dp[i-1][w-weights[i]]). Space optimize to 1D.
what_interviewers_want: Why iterate w backwards in 1D version. Difference from unbounded knapsack (coin change).
python_code:
def knapsack(W, weights, values):
    n = len(weights)
    dp = [0]*(W+1)
    for i in range(n):
        for w in range(W, weights[i]-1, -1):  # reverse!
            dp[w] = max(dp[w], values[i]+dp[w-weights[i]])
    return dp[W]

title: Number of Islands
description: Count islands in a 2D binary grid using BFS/DFS.
dry_run: Iterate grid. On '1': count++, BFS flood-fill marking visited. Next unvisited '1' is new island.
approach: DFS in-place (mark '0'). BFS with visited set. Union-Find (most impressive).
what_interviewers_want: Union-Find approach. Handling grid as implicit graph. Connected components in general.

title: Course Schedule
description: Detect cycle in directed graph to determine if all courses can be completed.
dry_run: 5 courses, prereqs=[[1,0],[2,1],[3,2]]. Build adj list. Kahn's BFS: indegree[0]=0→process. Indegree updates…
approach: Kahn's BFS (topological sort) or DFS with 3-color marking. O(V+E).
what_interviewers_want: Kahn's vs DFS tradeoffs. Topological order output. Detect specific cycle (LC 802).

title: Word Ladder
description: Find shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord changing one letter at a time.
dry_run: BFS level by level. For each word, generate all 1-char mutations. Check if in wordSet. Shortest path = level count.
approach: BFS on implicit graph. Bidirectional BFS halves search space — key optimization.
what_interviewers_want: Bidirectional BFS. Pre-building adjacency (pattern→words). O(n×m²) with optimization.

title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
description: Find shortest path from source to all nodes in weighted graph.
dry_run: Graph with 5 nodes. Min-heap starts with (0,src). Pop min-dist node, relax neighbors, push updated.
approach: Min-heap (priority queue). dist[] array. Only process each node once. O((V+E) log V).
what_interviewers_want: Why Dijkstra fails with negative edges (Bellman-Ford instead). Real use: Google Maps, ride-sharing ETA.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description: Determine if a binary tree is a valid BST.
dry_run: Pass min/max bounds down. Node 5: valid in (-inf,inf). Left child 3: valid in (-inf,5). Right child 7: valid in (5,inf).
approach: Recursive with (node, min_val, max_val). O(n) time O(h) space.
what_interviewers_want: Why just comparing left<root<right is wrong. Inorder traversal approach. BST iterator.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of Binary Tree
description: Find LCA of two nodes p and q in a binary tree.
dry_run: DFS: if node is p or q, return node. LCA is where left and right both return non-null.
approach: Single DFS. If both subtrees return non-null, current node is LCA. O(n).
what_interviewers_want: BST vs general tree difference. Without parent pointers. With parent pointers (different approach).
python_code:
def lowestCommonAncestor(root, p, q):
    if not root or root == p or root == q: return root
    left = lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q)
    right = lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q)
    return root if left and right else left or right

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design algorithm to serialize/deserialize a binary tree.
dry_run: BFS serialize: '1,2,3,null,null,4,5'. Deserialize: rebuild level by level using queue.
approach: BFS with null markers. Or preorder DFS. Codec must handle null nodes explicitly.
what_interviewers_want: Why preorder works but inorder alone doesn't. Handle null encoding. Self-balancing BSTs.

title: Kth Largest Element in Array
description: Find the kth largest element without fully sorting.
dry_run: Min-heap of size k. For [3,2,1,5,6,4],k=2: heap=[3,2]→push5 pop2→[3,5]→push6 pop3→[5,6]→push4 pop4→[5,6]. top=5.
approach: Min-heap of size k: O(n log k). QuickSelect: O(n) average, O(n²) worst.
what_interviewers_want: QuickSelect deep-dive with randomized pivot. When heap wins (streaming). When QuickSelect wins.

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
description: Merge k sorted linked lists and return one sorted list.
dry_run: Min-heap initialized with head of each list. Pop min, add next node from that list. O(N log k).
approach: Min-heap of (val, list_idx, node). Each pop gives next smallest. O(N log k) where N=total nodes.
what_interviewers_want: Why divide-conquer is also O(N log k). Heap vs divide-conquer for different k sizes.

title: Daily Temperatures
description: For each day, how many days to wait for a warmer temperature?
approach: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Pop when warmer found. O(n).
what_interviewers_want: Pattern: next greater element. Applies to stock span, asteroid collision, building view problems.

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
description: Find largest rectangle area in histogram.
dry_run: heights=[2,1,5,6,2,3]. Monotonic increasing stack. On decrease: pop and compute area using current index and new stack top as boundaries.
approach: Monotonic increasing stack. Width = curr_idx - stack[-1] - 1 after pop. Sentinel 0 at end.
what_interviewers_want: Width calculation formula when popping. Maximal rectangle in binary matrix (extension).
python_code:
def largestRectangleArea(heights):
    stack = [-1]
    heights.append(0)
    res = 0
    for i,h in enumerate(heights):
        while stack[-1]!=-1 and heights[stack[-1]]>=h:
            height = heights[stack.pop()]
            width = i - stack[-1] - 1
            res = max(res, height*width)
        stack.append(i)
    return res

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Reverse a singly linked list iteratively and recursively.
dry_run: 1→2→3→4→5. prev=None,curr=1. next=2,1.next=None,prev=1,curr=2. Repeat. prev=5 (new head).
approach: Three-pointer iterative O(n) O(1). Recursive O(n) O(n) stack.
what_interviewers_want: Both iterative and recursive. Reverse in groups of k (LC 25). In-place requirement.
python_code:
def reverseList(head):
    prev = None
    while head:
        nxt = head.next
        head.next = prev
        prev = head
        head = nxt
    return prev

title: LRU Cache
description: Design LRU Cache with get and put in O(1).
dry_run: capacity=2. put(1,1),put(2,2). get(1)→1. put(3,3) evicts key 2 (LRU). get(2)→-1.
approach: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. Map keys to nodes. DLL maintains recency order. O(1) ops.
what_interviewers_want: Why doubly linked + hashmap gives O(1). LFU cache variant. Thread-safe version.

title: Design URL Shortener (bit.ly)
description: 100M URLs/day. Shorten long URLs, redirect on access, analytics.
dry_run: Encode: MD5/base62 of URL → 7-char code. Store longURL→shortCode in Cassandra. Redirect: 301 vs 302 tradeoff.
approach: API→LB→App servers→Redis(hot URLs)→Cassandra(KV). Separate analytics Kafka stream.
what_interviewers_want: 301(permanent,browser caches,less server load) vs 302(temporary,can track clicks). Hash collision. Custom aliases. Expiry.

title: Design Rate Limiter
description: Limit API to 100 req/min per user. Distributed across nodes.
dry_run: Token bucket: Redis INCR on key user:1001:minute:1430. If count>100, reject. TTL=60s.
approach: Token bucket/sliding window counter in Redis. Lua script for atomic INCR+TTL. API gateway layer.
what_interviewers_want: 4 algorithms comparison (fixed window/sliding log/sliding counter/token/leaky). Race conditions. Multi-datacenter.

title: Design Instagram News Feed
description: 500M DAU. Generate personalized feed. Handle celebrities (>1M followers).
dry_run: Push model: on post→fan-out to all follower feed caches. Pull for celebrities. Hybrid: push for ≤1k followers.
approach: Post service→Kafka→FanOut service→Redis sorted set (score=timestamp). CDN for media. ML ranking layer.
what_interviewers_want: Celebrity problem threshold. Feed ranking (engagement signals). Pagination cursor. Cold start for new users.

title: Design WhatsApp Messaging
description: 2B users, real-time messaging, group chats up to 1024 members, offline delivery.
dry_run: WebSocket per user via connection server. Msg→Kafka→fanout→recipients. Store in Cassandra. Push notification if offline.
approach: WebSocket servers (stateful)→message router→Kafka→delivery workers→Cassandra. Presence service. E2E encryption.
what_interviewers_want: E2E encryption (Signal protocol). Offline message store + delivery. Group chat fanout strategy. Read receipts at scale.

title: Design YouTube
description: Video upload, transcoding, streaming at global scale. 500h video uploaded/min.
dry_run: Upload→S3 raw→SQS→transcoding workers (FFmpeg)→multiple resolutions→S3→CDN. HLS/DASH for adaptive streaming.
approach: Pre-signed S3 upload. Async transcode queue. CDN edge caching. Metadata: MySQL. Views: Kafka+batch. Recommendations: ML.
what_interviewers_want: Adaptive bitrate (HLS). CDN pop selection. View count eventual consistency. Copyright detection (YouTube ID).

title: Design Notification System
description: Send push/email/SMS to 1B users. Priority channels, user preferences, retry.
dry_run: Event→Kafka topic per channel→consumer workers→FCM(push)/SES(email)/Twilio(SMS). Dead letter queue on failure.
approach: Kafka fan-out per channel. Template service. Preference/opt-out service. Dedup service (idempotency key).
what_interviewers_want: Idempotency (no duplicate notifications). Priority queues (OTP > marketing). Rate limiting per user per day.

title: Design Distributed Cache (Redis-like)
description: Build Redis-like distributed cache. Consistent hashing, replication, eviction.
dry_run: Client hashes key to node via consistent hash ring. Virtual nodes handle uneven distribution. Write to primary+2 replicas.
approach: Consistent hashing ring with virtual nodes. Replication factor=3. LRU/LFU eviction. AOF+RDB persistence.
what_interviewers_want: Consistent hashing vs modulo (node addition). Cache stampede (mutex/probabilistic early expiry). Write-through vs write-back.

title: Design Search Autocomplete
description: Typeahead for search box. Top-5 suggestions in <100ms. 10M queries/day.
dry_run: Trie with top-k at each node. Redis cache for common prefixes. Offline log analysis updates frequencies.
approach: Trie + top-k per prefix (Redis). Offline: Kafka logs→Spark aggregation→update trie. Sharded by prefix.
what_interviewers_want: Trie vs inverted index. Sharding strategy (prefix-based). Personalization. Multi-language support.

title: LLD: Parking Lot System
description: Design parking lot with multiple floors, spot types, ticketing.
dry_run: Classes: ParkingLot(Singleton)→Floor→Spot(Compact/Large/Handicapped)→Vehicle→Ticket. Strategy for spot selection.
approach: Strategy pattern (spot selection). Observer (display boards). Factory (vehicle types). Singleton (ParkingLot).
what_interviewers_want: SOLID principles. Concurrency for spot reservation (synchronized/locks). Extensibility for new spot types.

title: LLD: Elevator System
description: Multiple elevators with intelligent scheduling. SCAN algorithm.
dry_run: State machine: IDLE/MOVING_UP/MOVING_DOWN. SCAN: serve all requests in one direction, then reverse.
approach: State machine per elevator. Scheduler (SCAN/FCFS). Observer pattern for display. Command pattern for requests.
what_interviewers_want: SCAN vs FCFS. Emergency stop. Concurrency for button presses. Energy efficiency optimization.

title: LLD: Splitwise (Expense Splitting)
description: Split expenses among users with EQUAL, EXACT, PERCENT, SHARES methods.
dry_run: Group: 3 users. Expense ₹300 EQUAL: each owes ₹100. Balance graph: A→B=100, B→C=50. Simplify debts.
approach: Strategy pattern for split types. Balance graph simplification (greedy: match max creditor with max debtor).
what_interviewers_want: Debt simplification algorithm. Transaction history. Multi-currency. Notification on settlement.

title: LLD: Chess Game
description: Design a 2-player chess game with all piece movements and check detection.
dry_run: Board[8][8] of Piece. Each Piece subclass implements getValidMoves(). Move validates check. Check/checkmate detection.
approach: Piece hierarchy (abstract→King/Queen/Rook/Bishop/Knight/Pawn). Board as 2D array. Game class manages turns.
what_interviewers_want: Castling, en passant, promotion. Check detection without moving. Undo move (Command pattern). AI opponent hook.

title: Process vs Thread — OS Fundamentals
description: Core OS interview question for product companies. Process isolation, thread sharing, context switch.
dry_run: Process: own memory space, file descriptors, PID. Thread: shared heap, own stack+registers. Context switch: save/restore PCB.
approach: Process=program in execution. Thread=unit of execution within process. IPC mechanisms. Synchronization primitives.
what_interviewers_want: When to use threads vs processes. Race conditions. Deadlock conditions (MHCW). Real example in their codebase.

title: Virtual Memory and Paging
description: Virtual address space, page tables, TLB, page faults, demand paging.
dry_run: Virtual address → page table → physical frame. TLB hit: direct physical. TLB miss: walk page table. Page fault: load from disk.
approach: Demand paging. Page replacement (LRU, Clock). Working set model. Thrashing.
what_interviewers_want: Why virtual memory matters (isolation, larger address space). Thrashing and how to detect. Modern OS (huge pages).

title: ACID Transactions and Isolation Levels
description: ACID properties, isolation levels, dirty reads, phantom reads, serializable.
dry_run: Read Uncommitted→Read Committed→Repeatable Read→Serializable. Each level prevents: dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads.
approach: ACID: Atomicity(rollback), Consistency(constraints), Isolation(concurrency), Durability(WAL). MVCC for isolation.
what_interviewers_want: Which isolation level does MySQL default to (Repeatable Read). When to use Serializable. MVCC vs lock-based.

title: Database Indexing — B-Tree vs Hash Index
description: B-Tree index, composite indexes, covering indexes, index selectivity.
dry_run: B-Tree: sorted, range queries O(log n). Hash: O(1) exact match, no range. Composite (a,b,c): leftmost prefix rule.
approach: Clustered vs non-clustered. Index selectivity. Query explain plan. When not to index.
what_interviewers_want: Why B-Tree not Binary Tree (disk page size, fan-out). Composite index column order. Index bloat.

title: HTTP vs HTTPS — How TLS Works
description: TLS handshake, symmetric vs asymmetric encryption, certificates, HSTS.
dry_run: ClientHello→ServerHello+Certificate→ClientKeyExchange(premaster secret encrypted with pubkey)→session keys derived→Application data.
approach: Asymmetric for key exchange, symmetric for data. Certificate chain of trust. TLS 1.3 improvements.
what_interviewers_want: Why HTTPS doesn't prevent DDoS. Certificate pinning. MITM attack scenario. How browsers verify certs.
